prompt:
IT asset-management ticket, PowerShell. Our CMDB import needs a nightly Meraki inventory pull and the quick script someone wrote is a liability: it lets Invoke-RestMethod follow redirects on its own (which mangles the auth header), it ignores rate limiting, and its output property order changes run to run so every diff is noise. I want a proper pwsh module — MerakiPages.psm1 plus at least two dot-sourced implementation files (transport / paging / inventory, your call on the split) — that talks to the Cisco Meraki Dashboard API v1 correctly. test_meraki_pages.ps1 is the acceptance contract — `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_meraki_pages.ps1` runs everything against loopback fake dashboards; no real Meraki org, no real key, and never a real sleep (waiting goes through an injected SleepAction).

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Cisco Meraki documentation — at minimum the Dashboard API v1 Authentication page (the standard bearer header), the Pagination page (perPage/startingAfter and the RFC 5988 Link header relations), the Rate Limit page (429 + Retry-After), Meraki's first-party guidance on how api.meraki.com redirects API calls to dashboard shards and what that does to the Authorization header in common HTTP clients, and the organization networks and organization devices operation pages. Only first-party Cisco/Meraki pages count as sources. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json — protected like the test file; if current docs contradict anything pinned, report the conflict in your summary instead of editing protected files. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Functions the tests call (Set-StrictMode Latest throughout, ordinal string handling, no Format-* in data paths):
- New-MerakiContext -BaseUri -ApiKey [-TrustedHosts string[]] [-RetryLimit int] [-SleepAction scriptblock] → context object exposing BaseUri (trailing slash trimmed), ApiKey, TrustedHosts, RetryLimit (default 3), SleepAction. Default TrustedHosts must be the documented Meraki host-family suffixes (global, Canada, China, India, FedRAMP dashboards).
- Invoke-MerakiApi -Context -Path (or -Uri for absolute URLs) → object with StatusCode, Body (parsed JSON), LinkHeader. Sends the documented bearer header, follows redirects ITSELF (auto-redirect off): a redirect target whose host matches a trusted suffix (or whose host:port authority was explicitly injected) keeps the Authorization header, any other target is fetched WITHOUT it. On 429, wait exactly the Retry-After seconds via SleepAction and retry at most RetryLimit times, then throw naming the status. Non-2xx responses throw with the status and the messages from the documented errors envelope — never the key.
- ConvertFrom-LinkHeader -Value → hashtable of rel → URL handling both quoted and unquoted rel forms.
- Get-MerakiPaged -Context -Path [-PerPage int] → concatenated items across pages, following the Link next relation URL VERBATIM (no rebuilt query strings).
- Get-MerakiInventory -Context -OrganizationId [-PerPage int] → paged networks + devices for the org; ConvertTo-MerakiInventoryJson -Inventory → compact single-line JSON, byte-stable: organizationId, then networks [{id, name, productTypes}] sorted by id (ordinal), then devices [{serial, name, model, networkId, productType}] sorted by serial (ordinal), productTypes sorted ordinal — exactly the key order the tests pin.

test_meraki_pages.ps1 and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Meraki Dashboard API v1 REST (organization networks and devices, Link pagination, shard redirects)

official_sources.title: Authentication - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: v1 authenticates with the standard Authorization header valued 'Bearer <API key>'; v0's X-Cisco-Meraki-API-Key header must not be used; API keys are secrets that must never be logged

official_sources.title: How to Use the Cisco Meraki Dashboard API - Cisco Meraki Documentation
official_sources.used_for: api.meraki.com redirects calls to the organization's dashboard shard; a GET redirects with 302 and non-GETs with 307/308; many HTTP clients drop the Authorization header or substitute GET on redirect, so clients must handle redirects deliberately; 429 responses set the Retry-After header

official_sources.title: Pagination - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: perPage/startingAfter/endingBefore parameters and the RFC 5988 Link response header carrying up to first, prev, next, last relations whose URLs should be used directly rather than rebuilt

official_sources.title: Rate Limit - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: 10 requests per second per organization; HTTP 429 with Retry-After seconds and body {"errors": ["API rate limit exceeded for organization"]}

official_sources.title: Get Organization Networks - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations/{organizationId}/networks (perPage 3-100000) returning id, name, productTypes per network

official_sources.title: Get Organization Devices - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations/{organizationId}/devices (perPage 3-5000, default 1000) returning serial, name, model, networkId, productType, mac per device

research.language_rationale: The CMDB import tooling this feeds is a PowerShell shop. The Dashboard API is plain REST with bearer auth, Link-header pagination and shard redirects, and Meraki publishes no official PowerShell module, so an idiomatic pwsh module over the documented v1 REST contract (with explicit redirect and Retry-After handling that PowerShell's default web cmdlets get wrong) is the credible integration path.
